1019		}
1020		return fs, nil
1021	}
1022	
1023	// newFECReceiver builds a fresh FEC receive plane (decoder + residual-loss estimator) from
1024	// the bind-wide fecCfg, or returns (nil, nil) when FEC is disabled. It reads only immutable
1025	// post-construction config (m.fecCfg), so it is safe to call WITHOUT m.mu — which the
1026	// lock-free lazy receive-side instantiation on first authenticated binding
1027	// (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated) relies on. The decoder tuning matches what Open pins for
1028	// the primary: a retain window, and a recovery deadline past which a doomed group is folded
1029	// into the unrecoverable counter (D24), so a lazily-instantiated concentrator peer recovers
1030	// identically to an eagerly-opened one.
1031	func (m *Multipath) newFECReceiver() (*fecReceiver, error) {
1032		if m.fecCfg == nil {
1033			return nil, nil
1034		}
1035		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(*m.fecCfg)
1036		if err != nil {
1037			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC decoder: %w", err)
1038		}
1039		dec.SetRetainWindow(fecRetainGroups)
1040		dec.SetClock(fec.SystemClock{})
1041		dec.SetRecoveryDeadline(m.fecCfg.Deadline + resequencerTimeout)
1042		return &fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)}, nil
1043	}
1044	
1045	// ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated lazily builds a peer's HEAVY receive-side datapath — the
1046	// ~2048-frame resequencer ring and (when FEC is configured) the decoder's per-group buffers —
1047	// on the FIRST authenticated source->peer binding, rather than eagerly at Open (Q26). A
1048	// configured concentrator peer that has never been reached therefore carries none of that
1049	// per-peer memory; it materialises only once an authenticated PROBE has bound a source to it,
1050	// and is reclaimed on teardown (teardownPeerLocked), re-materialising cleanly on the next
1051	// re-bind. It publishes through the same atomic.Pointer the receive fast path Loads and runs
1052	// on the Bind-owned readLoop goroutine, which must never take m.mu (Close waits on the readers
1053	// WHILE holding it) — so it takes ONLY the per-peer lifecycleMu, never m.mu. That lifecycleMu
1054	// makes the whole build-and-publish of the heavy trio (resequencer + FEC receive AND send
1055	// planes) mutually exclusive with teardownPeerLocked's clearing of the same trio: a teardown
1056	// can no longer interleave between the resequencer publish and the FEC publish and leave a
1057	// half-published plane, nor resurrect a plane on a torn-down peer. It is idempotent (the
1058	// lifecycleMu-guarded resequencer==nil double-check elects a single instantiator when two
1059	// sockets bind the same peer concurrently; the loser returns without building). It re-instates
1060	// the SEND plane symmetric to the receive plane, so a torn-down-then-rebound FEC peer sends
1061	// parity again rather than silently sending unprotected. A build error on either FEC plane is a
1062	// programmer error (the ratios were validated in NewMultipath) — the resequencer is still
1063	// installed so DATA flows, only FEC is absent, which is the safe degradation.
1064	func (m *Multipath) ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(ps *peerState) {
1065		if ps.resequencer.Load() != nil {
1066			return // fast path: already instantiated (the eager primary, or a prior binding)
1067		}
1068		ps.lifecycleMu.Lock()
1069		defer ps.lifecycleMu.Unlock()
1070		if ps.resequencer.Load() != nil {
1071			return // a concurrent bind instantiated it while we waited on lifecycleMu
1072		}
1073		// Build both FEC planes BEFORE publishing anything; on a build error degrade to no-FEC (the
1074		// resequencer alone still carries DATA). Store the resequencer LAST: it is the election
1075		// sentinel the fast-path check above and the receive fast path key on, so a concurrent
1076		// reader never observes a live resequencer whose FEC planes are not yet published.
1077		fr, ferr := m.newFECReceiver()
1078		if ferr != nil {
1079			fr = nil
1080		}
1081		fs, serr := m.newFECSender()
1082		if serr != nil {
1083			fs = nil
1084		}
1085		if fr != nil {
1086			ps.fecRecv.Store(fr)
1087		}
1088		if fs != nil {
1089			ps.fecSend.Store(fs)
1090		}
1091		ps.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
1092	}
1093	
1094	// readLoop is one Bind-OWNED per-path receive goroutine (T30). It reads the path
1095	// socket and dispatches every datagram through handleInbound — which pushes DATA
1096	// into the shared resequencer and answers/consumes PROBEs — then pokes the delivery
1097	// signal so the single engine-facing drainer wakes to release any newly in-order
1098	// frame. The read buffer is private to this goroutine, so the per-path Codec's
1099	// scratch is never shared.
1100	//
1101	// Owning the readers in the Bind (rather than returning one ReceiveFunc per path to
1102	// the engine, as T12 did) is what makes a runtime path add/remove possible without
1103	// disturbing the engine: the engine builds its receive goroutines ONCE from Open's
1104	// return, so a path added later gets a reader HERE while the engine's fixed receive
1105	// set — and thus the WG session — sees no churn. The goroutine exits when its socket
1106	// is closed (RemovePath drains one path; Close drains all).
1107	func (m *Multipath) readLoop(ps *peerPathState, deliver chan<- struct{}) {
1108		defer m.readersWG.Done()
1109		readBuf := make([]byte, maxDatagram)
1110		for {
1111			n, srcAP, err := ps.conn.ReadFromUDPAddrPort(readBuf)
1112			if err != nil {
1113				return // socket closed: this path was removed, or the bind was closed
1114			}
1115			// Per-path received-wire accounting (T23): count the OUTER datagram this path
1116			// pulled off its socket before dispatch, lock-free. This goroutine is the sole
1117			// writer of ps.rxBytes, so the atomic Add is uncontended.
1118			ps.rxBytes.Add(uint64(n))
1119			m.demuxInbound(ps, readBuf[:n], srcAP)
1120			// Advance liveness off the receive path (throttled): a live signal on THIS path
1121			// is what lets a DIFFERENT, silent path be marked DOWN promptly even when the
1122			// probe-loop ticker is starved under load (D15). The peer probes every path
1123			// (and floods the survivor after it roams), so the surviving path's reader
1124			// supplies exactly that signal.
1125			m.tickLivenessFromReceive(time.Now())
1126			// Non-blocking poke: a single buffered slot coalesces bursts; the drainer
1127			// re-checks the resequencer on every wake, so a coalesced poke loses nothing.
1128			select {
1129			case deliver <- struct{}{}:
1130			default:
1131			}
1132		}
1133	}
1134	
1135	// tickLivenessFromReceive re-evaluates EVERY path's liveness from a receive
1136	// goroutine, throttled to at most once per probe interval (T39, defect D15). It is
1137	// the starvation-robust companion to StartProbeLoop's timer-driven Tick: the timer
1138	// goroutine can be delayed ~1s under CPU saturation, but the receive goroutines are
1139	// scheduled by the very traffic that must trigger failover, so liveness advances
1140	// regardless. Ticking is monotone-safe: Tick only marks an UP path DOWN once its
1141	// silence STRICTLY exceeds DownAfter and never brings a path UP (that needs
1142	// RecordEcho), so a more frequent Tick can only make a genuine DOWN transition land
1143	// sooner — never a premature/false one — and the failback hysteresis (owned by the
1144	// scheduler) is untouched. No-op when the probe transport is absent (interval unset).
1145	func (m *Multipath) tickLivenessFromReceive(now time.Time) {
1146		interval := m.sweepIntervalNanos.Load()
1147		if interval == 0 {
1148			return // no probe transport / loop not started: nothing to Tick
1149		}
1150		n := now.UnixNano()
1151		last := m.lastSweepNanos.Load()
1152		if n-last < interval {
1153			return // throttled: this interval's sweep was already taken
1154		}
1155		if !m.lastSweepNanos.CompareAndSwap(last, n) {
1156			return // another receive goroutine won this interval's sweep
1157		}
1158		// Snapshot the prober set under m.mu (a runtime AddPath/RemovePath mutates it),
1159		// then Tick OUTSIDE the lock so a transition's log write never runs under m.mu.
1160		// TryLock, NOT Lock: Close holds m.mu WHILE it waits on readersWG for the readers to
1161		// exit, so a reader that BLOCKED here on m.mu would deadlock that shutdown. The sweep
1162		// is opportunistic and throttled, so when the lock is contended (a concurrent
1163		// Close/AddPath/RemovePath/Send) simply skipping this interval is harmless — the
1164		// probe-loop ticker and the next receive still advance liveness. This preserves
1165		// Close's invariant that a reader never blocks on m.mu. The lock, when taken, is held
1166		// at most once per interval (~5/s) for a bounded snapshot, so it adds negligible
1167		// contention to Send and does not disturb the lock-free receive fast path.
1168		if !m.mu.TryLock() {
1169			return
1170		}
1171		// Sweep EVERY bound peer's paths (T93): the receive-tick liveness signal must advance each
1172		// peer's own probers so a concentrator peer's silent path is marked DOWN promptly even when
1173		// the probe-loop ticker is starved. On the single-peer edge/hub m.peers holds only the
1174		// primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split single-peer sweep.
1175		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, 0, len(m.paths))
1176		for _, p := range m.peers {
1177			for _, ps := range p.paths {
1178				if ps.prober != nil {
1179					probers = append(probers, ps.prober)
1180				}
1181			}
1182		}
1183		m.mu.Unlock()
1184		for _, pr := range probers {
1185			pr.Tick()
1186		}
1187		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18, the repeated-flap wedge). The active-backup
1188		// selection is otherwise recomputed ONLY inside the scheduler's Pick, which the
1189		// Bind calls ONLY from Send — so failover is pull-based: it happens only when the
1190		// engine hands down an egress datagram. When the ACTIVE path dies during an egress
1191		// LULL that is fatal: a repeated-flap kill landing on the just-restored primary
1192		// before the saturating flow re-fills it (the failback and the next kill overlap)
1193		// leaves both TCP directions stalled on the now-dead path, so NO Send occurs, so
1194		// Pick is never called, so egress is never switched to the healthy backup — the
1195		// bond wedges on the dead path until the 25s WG keepalive finally drives a Send.
1196		// The receive tick is the starvation-robust signal that ALREADY detected the DOWN
1197		// (it just Ticked the path down above); recomputing the scheduler from it makes the
1198		// switch EAGER — bounded by the detection window (~DownAfter + one interval) rather
1199		// than by the next application Send. Pick recomputes purely against current liveness
1200		// and the clock and the failback dwell is time-based, so a more frequent recompute
1201		// can only make a genuine transition land sooner — never a premature/false failover,
1202		// and never a shortened anti-thrash hysteresis. It takes ONLY the scheduler's own
1203		// lock (never m.mu), so it adds no receive-path m.mu contention and cannot invert
1204		// the Send-path m.mu→scheduler lock order.
1205		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
1206	}
1207	
1208	// nudgeSchedulerActive forces the scheduler to recompute its active egress path
1209	// against current liveness, independent of any application Send. It is the eager-
1210	// failover companion to the Send-driven Pick: driven from the liveness-detection
1211	// paths (the receive tick and the probe loop) it switches egress to a healthy backup
1212	// the moment the active path is detected DOWN, so a dead active path with stalled
1213	// egress does not wedge the bond until the next Send (defect D18). The returned index
1214	// is intentionally discarded — only the recompute (and its logged transition) is
1215	// wanted here; Send remains the sole reader of the selection for actual routing. Pick
1216	// is internally synchronized and never calls back into the Bind, so this is safe to
1217	// call from a receive goroutine or the probe loop without m.mu.
1218	//
1219	// It calls Recompute, NOT Pick: the nudge wants only the liveness-driven active-set
1220	// recompute (and its logged transition), and a weighted/aggregating scheduler's Pick
1221	// is STATEFUL — a spurious Pick here would consume a distribution slot and skew the
1222	// per-path weight split. Recompute is the non-consuming half that refreshes the
1223	// eligible/active set without touching distribution/pacing/load state, so the T40
1224	// eager-failover guarantee holds for BOTH the active-backup and the weighted policy
1225	// (defect D18).
1226	func (m *Multipath) nudgeSchedulerActive() {
1227		// Recompute EVERY bound peer's active egress set (T93): each peer schedules over its OWN
1228		// paths, so a liveness DOWN on one peer's path must nudge THAT peer's scheduler. The peer
1229		// set is read lock-free through peersView (published under m.mu at construction / peer
1230		// registration and immutable during an Open span), so this stays off m.mu exactly as the
1231		// pre-split single Recompute did. On the single-peer edge/hub peersView holds only the
1232		// primary (p.scheduler == m.scheduler), so this is byte-identical to the pre-split nudge.
1233		if peers := m.peersView.Load(); peers != nil {
1234			for _, p := range *peers {
1235				p.scheduler.Recompute()
1236			}
1237			return
1238		}
1239		m.scheduler.Recompute()
1240	}
1241	
1242	// newReceiveFunc returns the SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc: it drains EACH bound peer's
1243	// resequencer in that peer's own outer-seq order and hands each inner datagram up stamped
1244	// with THAT peer's stable virtual endpoint (per-packet endpoint fill), so the engine
1245	// attributes return traffic to the right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's
1246	// virt (invariant A1: one virtual endpoint per peer). A path's reader (handleInbound) has
1247	// already routed each DATA frame to its OWNING peer's resequencer via the peerPathState's
1248	// ps.peer back-reference, so a shared socket serving many peers keeps each peer's stream
1249	// isolated; this drainer just fans the in-order releases back in. Because every path's
1250	// reader feeds one of these resequencers and a single drainer releases them, a path (or
1251	// peer) added or removed at runtime needs no change to the engine's receive set.
1252	//
1253	// Fairness: the peers are scanned round-robin from a rotating cursor so a saturated peer
1254	// cannot starve another peer's in-order releases (single-peer edge/hub: the cursor is a
1255	// no-op). When nothing is ready across ANY peer it parks until a reader pokes deliver,
1256	// until Close closes closed, or until a short poll elapses — the poll guarantees a
1257	// head-of-line-blocked run still makes timeout progress even if the last live path fell
1258	// silent right after buffering it. A single drainer delivers with ZERO added reorder
1259	// (only it calls Pop), which is stricter than T12's per-path receivers.
1260	func (m *Multipath) newReceiveFunc(deliver <-chan struct{}, closed <-chan struct{}) ReceiveFunc {
1261		// One reusable poll timer per drainer, NOT a fresh time.After timer per park: the
1262		// drainer parks on every empty receive, and a per-park 250 ms timer — retained by
1263		// the runtime timer heap until it fires even after the park is over — added
1264		// avoidable allocation and GC pressure on the receive hot path (opus low defect).
1265		// The ReceiveFunc is driven by a single engine goroutine, so the timer has no
1266		// concurrent user; Stop+drain then Reset per park reuses the one allocation.
1267		timer := time.NewTimer(resequencerTimeout)
1268		stopTimer := func() {
1269			timer.Stop()
1270			select {
1271			case <-timer.C:
1272			default:
1273			}
1274		}
1275		stopTimer() // start inert; armed only while parked
1276		// rr is the round-robin cursor, advanced past a peer each time it yields a frame so
1277		// the next receive starts at the following peer. It is touched only by this single
1278		// engine goroutine, so it needs no synchronisation.
1279		var rr int
1280		return func(packets [][]byte, sizes []int, eps []Endpoint) (int, error) {
1281			for {
1282				// Scan every bound peer round-robin for an in-order resequenced DATA frame.
1283				// The item carries the outer source of the frame that produced it, so the
1284				// peer's virtual endpoint pins correctly even when the frame was buffered and
1285				// released out of arrival order. The peer view is read lock-free (peersView),
1286				// so a concurrent peer wiring/fan-out never contends m.mu here.
1287				peers := *m.peersView.Load()
1288				n := len(peers)
1289				progressed := false
1290				for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
1291					ps := peers[(rr+i)%n]
1292					rq := ps.resequencer.Load()
1293					if rq == nil {
1294						continue // a peer not yet Open on this span has no resequencer
1295					}
1296					it, ok := rq.Pop()
1297					if !ok {
1298						continue
1299					}
1300					rr = (rr + i + 1) % n // start the next scan after this peer (fairness)
1301					if len(it.Payload) > len(packets[0]) {
1302						// Oversize inner datagram: drop it, but a frame WAS dequeued this pass,
1303						// so keep draining (re-scan) rather than parking.
1304						progressed = true
1305						break
1306					}
1307					sizes[0] = copy(packets[0], it.Payload)
1308					eps[0] = m.virtualEndpoint(ps, it.Src)
1309					return 1, nil
1310				}
1311				if progressed {
1312					continue // dropped an oversize frame; re-scan before parking
1313				}
1314				timer.Reset(resequencerTimeout)
1315				select {
1316				case <-deliver:
1317					stopTimer()
1318				case <-timer.C:
1319					// Poll fired; its channel is already drained by this receive.
1320				case <-closed:
1321					stopTimer()
1322					return 0, errClosed
1323				}
1324			}
1325		}
1326	}
1327	
1328	// handleInbound decodes one received outer datagram UNDER THIS VIEW'S CODEC and dispatches
1329	// it by kind to the view's owning peer. It is the single per-frame receive action; the
1330	// readLoop reaches it through demuxInbound, which first resolves the owning view on a shared
1331	// concentrator socket (on the single-peer edge/hub the reader's own view is the owner, so
1332	// demuxInbound's fast path calls this directly — byte-identical to the pre-concentrator
1333	// behaviour). Delivery up the WG
1334	// path is deferred to the resequencer (Pop, in the engine-facing drainer): a DATA
1335	// frame is not handed up here but pushed into the shared resequencer to be released in
1336	// outer-seq order.
1337	//
1338	//   - DATA: the decoded inner datagram is pushed into the resequencer keyed by
1339	//     the frame's outer-seq (delivered later, in order, via Pop). The path's
1340	//     remote is NOT learned here — remote-learning is authenticated-only (see
1341	//     below), so a forged DATA frame cannot steer a path's return endpoint.
1342	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
1343	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
1344	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
1345	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
1346	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
1347	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
1348	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
1349	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
1350	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
1351	//
1352	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
1353	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
1354	// resolves D9: an attacker who forges an unauthenticated DATA frame can no longer
1355	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
1356	//
1357	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
1358	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
1359	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1360	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1361	// natively-received frame.
1362	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1363		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1364		if err != nil {
1365			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1366		}
1367		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1368	}
1369	
1370	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1371	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
1372	// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
1373	// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
1374	// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
1375	//
1376	// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
1377	// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
1378	// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
1379	// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
1380	//
1381	// Shared socket (>1 view):
1382	//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
1383	//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
1384	//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
1385	//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
1386	//     bounded by the static peer count). Only an authenticated PROBE establishes a binding
1387	//     (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned only from authenticated PROBEs), and a
1388	//     PROBE's MAC verifies under EXACTLY ONE psk — so the FIRST psk whose codec yields a PROBE
1389	//     identifies the peer, binds the source, dispatches, and the loop STOPS there. A non-PROBE
1390	//     decode does NOT stop the trial: DATA/PARITY carry no MAC and are forgeable by design, so
1391	//     a genuine PROBE from a later peer can cross-psk-garble into a DATA/PARITY kind under an
1392	//     earlier peer's codec (~0.4%); the loop must therefore `continue` past a non-PROBE decode
1393	//     to give every remaining psk its chance to authenticate a PROBE. A non-PROBE decode
1394	//     carries no binding authority and is dropped either way — a genuine DATA/PARITY from a
1395	//     not-yet-bound source never dispatches or binds until that peer's PROBE binds it, so
1396	//     continuing past it changes nothing for genuine frames. A forged/garbage frame verifies
1397	//     as a PROBE under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
1398	func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1399		views := ps.views.Load()
1400		if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
1401			m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
1402			return
1403		}
1404		if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
1405			for _, v := range *views {
1406				if v.peer == bound {
1407					m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
1408					return
1409				}
1410			}
1411			return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
1412		}
1413		for _, v := range *views {
1414			fr, err := v.codec.Decode(raw)
1415			if err != nil {
1416				continue // not this peer's psk — try the next
1417			}
1418			if _, isProbe := fr.(frame.Probe); !isProbe {
1419				// Decoded under this psk but not a PROBE (DATA/PARITY carry no MAC, so
1420				// this may be a genuine PROBE from a later peer that cross-garbled into an
1421				// unauthenticated kind here). No binding authority: try the remaining psks
1422				// rather than aborting — a genuine unbound DATA/PARITY still never binds.
1423				continue
1424			}
1425			if !m.bindSourceToPeer(srcAP.Addr(), v.peer) {
1426				// Provisional demux state is at its cap (bootstrap flood): drop this new source's
1427				// PROBE rather than grow the map or evict a live binding (Q26/Q27). WG retransmits
1428				// re-drive the bootstrap once a slot frees.
1429				return
1430			}
1431			// The binding just resolved this peer: lazily materialise its heavy receive datapath
1432			// (resequencer ring + FEC decoder buffers) BEFORE dispatch, so a configured peer that
1433			// had never been reached — and a peer whose state was torn down on session loss — pays
1434			// that memory only from its first authenticated binding onward (Q26).
1435			m.ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(v.peer)
1436			m.dispatchInbound(v, fr, raw, srcAP) // already decoded: dispatch without re-decoding
1437			return
1438		}
1439		// No peer's psk verified: a forged/garbage frame. No binding, drop.
1440	}
1441	
1442	// lookupPeerBySource resolves a learned source address to the peer bound to it, or nil when
1443	// the source is not yet bound. It reads the copy-on-write binding map with a single lock-free
1444	// Load, so the concentrator receive hot path never takes m.mu (see peerBySource).
1445	func (m *Multipath) lookupPeerBySource(addr netip.Addr) (*peerState, bool) {
1446		mp := m.peerBySource.Load()
1447		if mp == nil {
1448			return nil, false
1449		}
1450		p, ok := (*mp)[addr]
1451		return p, ok
1452	}
1453	
1454	// bindSourceToPeer records addr→p in the source-demux map, installed lock-free by a CAS
1455	// republish of a copy with the entry added (T88). It takes NO lock: a reader must never block
1456	// on m.mu (Close waits on the readers WHILE holding m.mu), so the binding — written from a
1457	// readLoop goroutine — must not acquire it. Idempotent: an already-present addr→p binding is a
1458	// no-op, and a lost CAS (a concurrent bind on another socket) simply retries. Copy-on-write
1459	// keeps every published map immutable, so a concurrent lookupPeerBySource over the old snapshot
1460	// is never disturbed.
1461	//
1462	// It returns true when addr is bound to p on return (either freshly installed, already present,
1463	// or re-pointed from another peer — a roam, T90) and false ONLY when the map is at its
1464	// maxDemuxSources cap and addr is a NEW source: drop-on-exhaustion (Q26/Q27). A cap-drop NEVER
1465	// evicts an existing binding, so a live peer is never disturbed by a bootstrap flood; only a
1466	// brand-new source's bootstrap is (temporarily) refused, and WG retransmits cover the gap once
1467	// a slot frees (e.g. a dead peer's teardown). Re-pointing or re-affirming an already-present
1468	// source does not grow the map and is therefore never blocked by the cap.
1469	func (m *Multipath) bindSourceToPeer(addr netip.Addr, p *peerState) bool {
1470		for {
1471			old := m.peerBySource.Load()
1472			var n int
1473			present := false
1474			if old != nil {
1475				existing, ok := (*old)[addr]
1476				if ok {
1477					present = true
1478					if existing == p {
1479						return true // already bound to p: idempotent no-op
1480					}
1481				}
1482				n = len(*old)
1483			}
1484			if !present && m.maxDemuxSources > 0 && n >= m.maxDemuxSources {
1485				// Cap exhausted and this is a NEW source: drop it. An existing (live) binding is
1486				// never evicted to make room — bootstrap degrades, isolation holds.
1487				return false
1488			}
1489			next := make(map[netip.Addr]*peerState, n+1)
1490			if old != nil {
1491				for k, v := range *old {
1492					next[k] = v
1493				}
1494			}
1495			next[addr] = p
1496			if m.peerBySource.CompareAndSwap(old, &next) {
1497				return true
1498			}
1499			// Lost the race with a concurrent bind: reload and retry.
1500		}
1501	}
1502	
1503	// unbindPeerSources removes every source->peer entry pointing at p from the demux map (a CAS
1504	// republish of a copy with p's entries dropped), reclaiming their cap slots so a fresh
1505	// authenticated PROBE can re-bind after the peer is re-instantiated. Like bindSourceToPeer it
1506	// is lock-free copy-on-write (retry on a lost CAS), so it composes with a concurrent bind on a
1507	// readLoop goroutine without either blocking on m.mu. A no-op when p holds no bindings.
1508	func (m *Multipath) unbindPeerSources(p *peerState) {
1509		for {
1510			old := m.peerBySource.Load()
1511			if old == nil {
1512				return
1513			}
1514			found := false
1515			for _, v := range *old {
1516				if v == p {
1517					found = true
1518					break
1519				}
1520			}
1521			if !found {
1522				return
1523			}
1524			next := make(map[netip.Addr]*peerState, len(*old))
1525			for k, v := range *old {
1526				if v != p {
1527					next[k] = v
1528				}
1529			}
1530			if m.peerBySource.CompareAndSwap(old, &next) {
1531				return
1532			}
1533		}
1534	}
1535	
1536	// peerIsLiveLocked reports whether ANY of peer p's paths is currently StateUp — the liveness
1537	// gate that makes teardown safe: a peer with a live path is actively carrying (or about to
1538	// carry) traffic and must NEVER be torn down (Q26). It reads each path's own immutable prober
1539	// State() (atomic internally, per the PathHealth contract), so it is safe under m.mu. A peer
1540	// with no prober-bearing path (a bind without the probe transport) reports not-live, matching
1541	// the fact that such a bind has no liveness signal to protect.
1542	func (m *Multipath) peerIsLiveLocked(p *peerState) bool {
1543		for _, pp := range p.paths {
1544			if pp.prober != nil && pp.prober.State() == telemetry.StateUp {
1545				return true
1546			}
1547		}
1548		return false
1549	}
1550	
1551	// teardownPeerLocked frees a dead peer's HEAVY per-peer state — the ~2048-frame resequencer
1552	// ring and the FEC send/receive buffers — and releases its source->peer demux bindings,
1553	// reclaiming both the memory and the demux-map cap slots (Q26). It is the lifecycle dual of
1554	// ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated: after teardown the peer is dormant (its light state — psk,
1555	// codec, reflector, per-(peer,path) views — survives so a trial-decode still authenticates it),
1556	// and the next authenticated PROBE re-binds a source and re-instantiates the ring cleanly. It
1557	// REFUSES to tear down a LIVE peer (any path StateUp) and the embedded primary (the edge/hub,
1558	// whose lifecycle is Open/Close, not session teardown), returning false in both cases so a
1559	// caller can distinguish "torn down" from "kept". The heavy fields are atomic.Pointer, so
1560	// Store(nil) is safe against a concurrent readLoop (which nil-guards its Load); the drainer
1561	// likewise skips a peer whose resequencer Loads nil. Caller holds m.mu.
1562	func (m *Multipath) teardownPeerLocked(p *peerState) bool {
1563		if p == m.peerState {
1564			return false // the primary (edge/hub) is torn down only by Close, never by session loss
1565		}
1566		if m.peerIsLiveLocked(p) {
1567			return false // a live (Up) peer is never torn down, whatever other peers' churn
1568		}
1569		// Clear the heavy trio under lifecycleMu so a concurrent ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated on a
1570		// readLoop (which also holds lifecycleMu across its whole build-and-publish) cannot interleave:
1571		// it either runs wholly before this clear (and is then wholly undone here) or wholly after
1572		// (and rebuilds all three cleanly). This closes the resurrection/half-published hole where a
1573		// teardown landing mid-instantiation left a fecRecv/fecSend without its resequencer, which the
1574		// next re-bind then reused stale. lifecycleMu is a leaf lock (see its field doc); we hold m.mu
1575		// here, giving the fixed order m.mu -> lifecycleMu with no cycle. The unbind runs after the
1576		// clear (and outside lifecycleMu): it is its own lock-free CAS loop and needs no ordering here.
1577		p.lifecycleMu.Lock()
1578		p.resequencer.Store(nil)
1579		p.fecRecv.Store(nil)
1580		p.fecSend.Store(nil)
1581		p.lifecycleMu.Unlock()
1582		m.unbindPeerSources(p)
1583		return true
1584	}
1585	
1586	// TearDownPeer frees the heavy per-peer state of the named configured peer once its WireGuard
1587	// session / liveness is gone — the device wires this from its per-peer session events (Q26). It
1588	// is a no-op returning false when the peer is unknown, is the embedded primary, or is still
1589	// LIVE (a live peer is never torn down); it returns true when the peer's resequencer ring and
1590	// FEC buffers were freed and its source bindings released. A torn-down configured peer
1591	// re-instantiates cleanly on its next authenticated PROBE (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated).
1592	func (m *Multipath) TearDownPeer(name string) bool {
1593		m.mu.Lock()
1594		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1595		p, ok := m.peersByName[name]
1596		if !ok {
1597			return false
1598		}
1599		return m.teardownPeerLocked(p)
1600	}
1601	
1602	// EverHadLivePath reports whether ANY configured path, for ANY bound peer, has EVER
1603	// reached liveness telemetry.StateUp since this Bind was constructed (I4). It is
1604	// STICKY: once true it stays true for the Bind's lifetime, even if every path later
1605	// goes down — a total outage AFTER connectivity was established is a genuine failure
1606	// signal, not a startup warmup. The device-package engineLogger adapter consults it to
1607	// gate the coalesced startup no-healthy-path INFO line (see ErrNoHealthyPath) to the
1608	// warmup window only. Safe for concurrent use (backed by atomic.Bool); never blocks.
1609	func (m *Multipath) EverHadLivePath() bool {
1610		return m.everUp.Load()
1611	}
1612	
1613	// dispatchInbound handles one already-decoded inbound frame on the resolved peer's view (ps):
1614	// it routes to that peer's resequencer / FEC decoder / reflector. The source demux in
1615	// demuxInbound has already selected ps so a shared socket serving many peers resequences each
1616	// peer's stream against that peer's own buffer; on the single-peer edge/hub ps.peer is the
1617	// embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton. raw is retained for
1618	// the probe transport (HandleEcho / Reflect re-decode it under the peer's psk).
1619	func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1620		pr := ps.peer
1621		switch f := fr.(type) {
1622		case frame.Data:
1623			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1624			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1625			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1626			if rq == nil {
1627				// The peer's heavy receive state is absent — not yet instantiated, or torn down on
1628				// session/liveness loss while a DATA frame was in flight on this readLoop. Drop it;
1629				// a fresh authenticated PROBE re-instantiates the ring before the next DATA lands.
1630				return
1631			}
1632			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1633				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1634				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1635				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1636				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1637				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1638				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1639				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1640				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1641				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1642				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1643				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1644				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1645					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1646				}
1647				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1648			} else {
1649				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1650			}
1651		case frame.Parity:
1652			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1653			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1654			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1655			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1656			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1657			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1658				rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1659				if rq == nil {
1660					return // heavy receive state torn down mid-flight (see the DATA case)
1661				}
1662				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1663				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1664				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1665			}
1666		case frame.Probe:
1667			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1668			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1669			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1670			if f.IsEcho {
1671				if ps.prober != nil {
1672					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1673					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1674					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1675					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1676					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1677					// Sticky "ever had a live path" latch (I4): a fresh echo that just brought
1678					// this path to StateUp (or found it already Up) flips everUp permanently.
1679					// Checked here rather than only in Liveness.transition so the bind-level
1680					// predicate needs no wiring through NewProber/NewLiveness — it observes the
1681					// SAME state HandleEcho just updated, at the one call site that can ever
1682					// change Down->Up.
1683					if ps.prober.State() == telemetry.StateUp {
1684						m.everUp.Store(true)
1685					}
1686				}
1687				return
1688			}
1689			if pr.reflector != nil {
1690				if echo, rerr := pr.reflector.Reflect(raw); rerr == nil {
1691					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe, so this receive-goroutine reflection
1692					// races no in-flight Send on the same socket.
1693					_, _ = ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(echo, srcAP)
1694				}
1695			}
1696		default:
1697			// CONTROL (and any unhandled kind) is not delivered up this path.
1698		}
1699	}
1700	
1701	// observeRecovered feeds every FEC-reconstructed data frame into the resequencer at
1702	// its ORIGINAL outer-seq (recovered from the shard's coded bytes) via the NON-resyncing
1703	// ObserveRecovered path, so a frame rebuilt from parity resequences identically to a
1704	// natively-received frame — filling the exact gap the resequencer would otherwise time
1705	// out on — WITHOUT a late batch of recovered seqs being able to move the release point
1706	// or dump the live buffer (recovery must never cause loss). Only frames actually placed
1707	// AHEAD of the release point advance the honest delivered-recovery counter; a frame
1708	// rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and not
1709	// counted, so /metrics reflects delivered recovery, not mere reconstruction. A
1710	// malformed recovered shard (too short to hold the outer-seq prefix) is dropped: it
1711	// signals an encoder/decoder mismatch, not a deliverable frame.
1712	func (m *Multipath) observeRecovered(fr *fecReceiver, rq *reseq.Resequencer, recovered []fec.Recovered, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1713		for _, rec := range recovered {
1714			seq, inner, err := splitFECShardPayload(rec.Payload)
1715			if err != nil {
1716				continue
1717			}
1718			// Residual-loss accounting (T29): FEC reconstructed this outer-seq, so it is NOT
1719			// residual loss — mark it present in the post-recovery estimator even if the
1720			// resequencer later drops it as late (that is a latency outcome, not a masking
1721			// failure; the P4 residual bound measures loss FEC failed to mask).
1722			if fr.connLoss != nil {
1723				fr.connLoss.Observe(seq)
1724			}
1725			if rq.ObserveRecovered(seq, inner, srcAP) {
1726				fr.deliveredRecovered.Add(1)
1727			}
1728		}
1729	}
1730	
1731	// virtualEndpoint returns the single stable endpoint the engine holds for the GIVEN
1732	// peer (ps). Each peer owns its OWN virtual endpoint (invariant A1: one virtual endpoint
1733	// per peer), so the drainer stamps a delivered inner datagram with the endpoint of the
1734	// peer whose resequencer released it — the engine thus attributes return traffic to the
1735	// right peer and Send routes replies back via that peer's virt. On a peer with no
1736	// configured endpoint (the concentrator) its destination is pinned ONCE to the first
1737	// learned source; thereafter every path returns the identical pointer so the engine sees
1738	// one peer, never per-packet churn.
1739	//
1740	// Hot-path note: the destination, once pinned, never changes, and it is published
1741	// through an atomic.Pointer (see udpEndpoint). So the common case takes a
1742	// lock-free fast path — every received datagram would otherwise contend m.mu with
1743	// in-flight Sends. The mutex is acquired only to pin the FIRST learned source.
1744	func (m *Multipath) virtualEndpoint(ps *peerState, learned netip.AddrPort) Endpoint {
1745		if ps.virt.dstValid() {
1746			return ps.virt
1747		}
1748		m.mu.Lock()
1749		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1750		if !ps.virt.dstValid() {
1751			ps.virt.setDst(learned)
1752		}
1753		return ps.virt
1754	}
1755	
1756	// Send wraps each buffer in an outer DATA frame (fresh outer-seq + the chosen
1757	// path's id) and writes it to that path's remote. The egress path is chosen by
1758	// the injected scheduler (T15): the active-backup policy returns the preferred
1759	// primary while it is up and the failover backup otherwise. The scheduler
1760	// selects by path priority/liveness only; the Bind additionally requires THAT
1761	// ONE chosen path to have a known remote, failing the send otherwise rather
1762	// than silently dropping.
1763	//
1764	// Behavioural change from pre-T15: this is a deliberate NARROWING. The removed
1765	// pickPathLocked iterated the paths and skipped any healthy-but-remoteless one,
1766	// falling through to the next healthy path WITH a known remote, so a send failed
1767	// only when NO path had a remote. Now the scheduler owns selection and returns a
1768	// single index; the Bind does NOT fall through, because a Bind-level fall-through
1769	// would bypass the scheduler's hysteresis (failover/failback). The residual
1770	// window this opens: if the scheduler's chosen path is reported Up but its remote
1771	// is not yet learned — e.g. a concentrator/hub restart, or a T16 NAT-rebind
1772	// before the first inbound packet re-teaches the remote — the send fails until
1773	// that path's remote is learned, even if another path has a known remote.
1774	//
1775	// Critical-section discipline: path selection and framing run under m.mu (the
1776	// send Codec is shared and stateful — D5's single keystream requires sequential,
1777	// mutex-guarded Encode), but each datagram is encoded into its OWN fresh buffer
1778	// so it outlives the lock, and the WriteToUDPAddrPort syscalls run WITHOUT m.mu
1779	// held. The scheduler is internally synchronized and never calls back into the
1780	// Bind, so calling Pick under m.mu cannot deadlock and adds no receive-path
1781	// contention (the lock-free virtual-endpoint fast path is untouched). A receive
1782	// goroutine pinning the virtual endpoint therefore never blocks behind an
1783	// in-flight transmit syscall.
1784	func (m *Multipath) Send(bufs [][]byte, ep Endpoint) error {
1785		ue, ok := ep.(*udpEndpoint)
1786		if !ok {
1787			return conn.ErrWrongEndpointType
1788		}
1789	
1790		// Classify the batch by inner WireGuard message type (defect D22): a handshake or
1791		// keepalive is passed to the scheduler as ClassControl so a pacing scheduler exempts
1792		// it from the per-path data token buckets and bulk overload cannot shed it and starve
1793		// rekey. The classifier is parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia profile, so it works
1794		// under advanced-security obfuscation (custom magic headers + handshake junk), not
1795		// only vanilla WireGuard. It reads only the (possibly junk-shifted) type word off the
1796		// lock. It is peer-agnostic (inner WireGuard type, not routing), so it runs before the
1797		// peer resolution below.
1798		class := m.classify.classifyBatch(bufs)
1799	
1800		m.mu.Lock()
1801		// Resolve the OWNING peer from the engine-facing virtual endpoint: each peer holds a
1802		// DISTINCT virt, so this datagram egresses on THAT peer's outerSeq, scheduler,
1803		// sendCodec, fecSend, and per-(peer,path) set — never the embedded primary's by
1804		// promotion. An endpoint of the right type but unknown to the demux (no bound peer)
1805		// cannot be routed, so it returns the no-path error rather than misrouting onto some
1806		// other peer's paths. On the single-peer edge/hub the primary's virt resolves to the
1807		// primary peerState, so behaviour is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton.
1808		peer, ok := m.peerByVirt[ue]
1809		if !ok {
1810			m.mu.Unlock()
1811			return ErrNoHealthyPath
1812		}
1813		if len(peer.paths) == 0 {
1814			m.mu.Unlock()
1815			return errClosed
1816		}
1817		idx := peer.scheduler.Pick(class)
1818		if idx == sched.PickPaced {
1819			// The scheduler shed this datagram for pacing while paths are healthy: drop it
1820			// (same as no-path), but surface a DISTINCT error so the diagnostic is not
1821			// conflated with a total outage. The rate-limited log is emitted at the source
1822			// (the scheduler), so no per-drop logging happens here on the hot path.
1823			m.mu.Unlock()
1824			return errPacerShedding
1825		}
1826		if idx < 0 || idx >= len(peer.paths) {
1827			m.mu.Unlock()
1828			return ErrNoHealthyPath
1829		}
1830		ps := peer.paths[idx]
1831		remote, ok := ps.getRemote()
1832		if !ok {
1833			m.mu.Unlock()
1834			return ErrNoHealthyPath
1835		}
1836		c := ps.conn
1837		// Snapshot this peer's send-FEC plane once under m.mu: a torn-down peer reads nil (sends
1838		// unprotected until re-bind rebuilds it), a re-bound peer reads its freshly re-instantiated
1839		// sender. The encoder is mutated (Admit) only here under m.mu, so this single Load pins a
1840		// stable sender for the whole batch.
1841		sendFEC := peer.fecSend.Load()
1842		wires := make([]fecWire, 0, len(bufs))
1843		for _, b := range bufs {
1844			seq := peer.outerSeq.Add(1)
1845			if sendFEC != nil {
1846				// FEC on (T24): admit the inner datagram (coded as seq || inner) to the group
1847				// encoder. The returned data shard rides a normal DATA frame carrying its FEC
1848				// group + shard index; when this admission FILLS the group the encoder returns
1849				// the group's parity shards, emitted here as KindParity frames on the SAME
1850				// chosen path. Spreading parity onto a DIFFERENT path than its data (so one
1851				// path outage cannot lose both) is a documented future refinement, deliberately
1852				// NOT implemented here (see the T24 design notes).
1853				ds, parity, err := sendFEC.enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, b))
1854				if err != nil {
1855					m.mu.Unlock()
1856					return err
1857				}
1858				wire, err := peer.sendCodec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: ps.id, FECGroup: uint32(ds.Group), FECIndex: uint8(ds.Index), Payload: b})
1859				if err != nil {
1860					m.mu.Unlock()
1861					return err
1862				}
1863				wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: wire})
1864				for _, par := range parity {
1865					pw, err := m.encodeParityLocked(peer, par, ps.id)
1866					if err != nil {
1867						m.mu.Unlock()
1868						return err
1869					}
1870					wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: pw, parity: true})
1871				}
1872				continue
1873			}
1874			wire, err := peer.sendCodec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: ps.id, Payload: b})
1875			if err != nil {
1876				m.mu.Unlock()
1877				return err
1878			}
1879			wires = append(wires, fecWire{b: wire})
1880		}
1881		fs := sendFEC
1882		m.mu.Unlock()
1883	
1884		for _, w := range wires {
1885			if _, err := c.WriteToUDPAddrPort(w.b, remote); err != nil {
1886				return err
1887			}
1888			// Per-path egress-wire accounting (T23): count the OUTER frame bytes just written
1889			// to this path (DATA and any FEC PARITY alike), lock-free and only for a datagram
1890			// that actually reached the socket. Send serialized the path choice under m.mu, so
1891			// ps is fixed here and this is the sole writer of ps.txBytes for this frame.
1892			ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1893			if fs != nil {
1894				// FEC frame accounting (T24/T25), counted only once the frame reached the socket
1895				// so the /metrics overhead ratio reflects wire cost actually spent. Parity and
1896				// DATA are charged to disjoint counters; their ratio ParityFrames/DataFrames is
1897				// the fixed-ratio overhead the P3 e2e asserts against M/K. fs != nil only when FEC
1898				// is enabled, so a plain (FEC-off) datapath increments neither counter.
1899				if w.parity {
1900					fs.parityFrames.Add(1)
1901					fs.parityBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1902				} else {
1903					fs.dataFrames.Add(1)
1904					// DATA-frame wire bytes: the overhead-BYTES denominator (T29). The P4
1905					// acceptance compares parity BYTES / data BYTES, so both are counted on the
1906					// same only-once-it-reached-the-socket basis as the frame counters.
1907					fs.dataBytes.Add(uint64(len(w.b)))
1908				}
1909			}
1910		}
1911		return nil
1912	}
1913	
1914	// fecWire is one framed outgoing datagram tagged with whether it is an FEC parity
1915	// frame, so the write loop can charge parity-overhead accounting (T24) without a
1916	// second pass.
1917	type fecWire struct {
1918		b      []byte

4	 */
5	
6	package device
7	
8	import (
9		"time"
10	)
11	
12	/* Specification constants */
13	
14	const (
15		RekeyAfterMessages      = (1 << 60)
16		RejectAfterMessages     = (1 << 64) - (1 << 13) - 1
17		RekeyAfterTime          = time.Second * 120
18		RekeyAttemptTime        = time.Second * 90
19		RekeyTimeout            = time.Second * 5
20		MaxTimerHandshakes      = 90 / 5 /* RekeyAttemptTime / RekeyTimeout */
21		RekeyTimeoutJitterMaxMs = 334
22		RejectAfterTime         = time.Second * 180
23		KeepaliveTimeout        = time.Second * 10
24		CookieRefreshTime       = time.Second * 120
25		HandshakeInitationRate  = time.Second / 50
26		PaddingMultiple         = 16
27	)
28	
29	const (
30		MinMessageSize = MessageKeepaliveSize                  // minimum size of transport message (keepalive)
31		MaxMessageSize = MaxSegmentSize                        // maximum size of transport message
32		MaxContentSize = MaxSegmentSize - MessageTransportSize // maximum size of transport message content
33	)
34	
35	/* Implementation constants */
36	
37	const (
38		UnderLoadAfterTime = time.Second // how long does the device remain under load after detected
39		MaxPeers           = 1 << 16     // maximum number of configured peers
40	)
41

1	/* SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
2	 *
3	 * Copyright (C) 2017-2023 WireGuard LLC. All Rights Reserved.
4	 */
5	
6	package device
7	
8	import (
9		"bytes"
10		"encoding/binary"
11		"errors"
12		"net"
13		"sync"
14		"time"
15	
16		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/conn"
17		"golang.org/x/crypto/chacha20poly1305"
18		"golang.org/x/net/ipv4"
19		"golang.org/x/net/ipv6"
20	)
21	
22	type QueueHandshakeElement struct {
23		msgType  uint32
24		packet   []byte
25		endpoint conn.Endpoint
26		buffer   *[MaxMessageSize]byte
27	}
28	
29	type QueueInboundElement struct {
30		buffer   *[MaxMessageSize]byte
31		packet   []byte
32		counter  uint64
33		keypair  *Keypair
34		endpoint conn.Endpoint
35	}
36	
37	type QueueInboundElementsContainer struct {
38		sync.Mutex
39		elems []*QueueInboundElement
40	}
41	
42	// clearPointers clears elem fields that contain pointers.
43	// This makes the garbage collector's life easier and
44	// avoids accidentally keeping other objects around unnecessarily.
45	// It also reduces the possible collateral damage from use-after-free bugs.
46	func (elem *QueueInboundElement) clearPointers() {
47		elem.buffer = nil
48		elem.packet = nil
49		elem.keypair = nil
50		elem.endpoint = nil
51	}
52	
53	/* Called when a new authenticated message has been received
54	 *
55	 * NOTE: Not thread safe, but called by sequential receiver!
56	 */
57	func (peer *Peer) keepKeyFreshReceiving() {
58		if peer.timers.sentLastMinuteHandshake.Load() {
59			return
60		}
61		keypair := peer.keypairs.Current()
62		if keypair != nil && keypair.isInitiator && time.Since(keypair.created) > (RejectAfterTime-KeepaliveTimeout-RekeyTimeout) {
63			peer.timers.sentLastMinuteHandshake.Store(true)
64			peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
65		}
66	}
67	
68	/* Receives incoming datagrams for the device
69	 *
70	 * Every time the bind is updated a new routine is started for
71	 * IPv4 and IPv6 (separately)
72	 */
73	func (device *Device) RoutineReceiveIncoming(
74		maxBatchSize int,
75		recv conn.ReceiveFunc,
76	) {
77		recvName := recv.PrettyName()
78		defer func() {
79			device.log.Verbosef("Routine: receive incoming %s - stopped", recvName)
80			device.queue.decryption.wg.Done()
81			device.queue.handshake.wg.Done()
82			device.net.stopping.Done()
83		}()
84	
85		device.log.Verbosef("Routine: receive incoming %s - started", recvName)
86	
87		// receive datagrams until conn is closed
88	
89		var (
90			bufsArrs    = make([]*[MaxMessageSize]byte, maxBatchSize)
91			bufs        = make([][]byte, maxBatchSize)
92			err         error
93			sizes       = make([]int, maxBatchSize)
94			count       int
95			endpoints   = make([]conn.Endpoint, maxBatchSize)
96			deathSpiral int
97			elemsByPeer = make(map[*Peer]*QueueInboundElementsContainer, maxBatchSize)
98		)
99	
100		for i := range bufsArrs {
101			bufsArrs[i] = device.GetMessageBuffer()
102			bufs[i] = bufsArrs[i][:]
103		}
104	
105		defer func() {
106			for i := 0; i < maxBatchSize; i++ {
107				if bufsArrs[i] != nil {
108					device.PutMessageBuffer(bufsArrs[i])
109				}
110			}
111		}()
112	
113		for {
114			count, err = recv(bufs, sizes, endpoints)
115			if err != nil {
116				if errors.Is(err, net.ErrClosed) {
117					return
118				}
119				device.log.Verbosef("Failed to receive %s packet: %v", recvName, err)
120				if neterr, ok := err.(net.Error); ok && !neterr.Temporary() {
121					return
122				}
123				if deathSpiral < 10 {
124					deathSpiral++
125					time.Sleep(time.Second / 3)
126					continue
127				}
128				return
129			}
130			deathSpiral = 0
131	
132			device.awg.ASecMux.RLock()
133			// handle each packet in the batch
134			for i, size := range sizes[:count] {
135				if size < MinMessageSize {
136					continue
137				}
138	
139				// check size of packet
140				packet := bufsArrs[i][:size]
141				var msgType uint32
142				if device.isAWG() {
143					// TODO:
144					// if awg.WaitResponse.ShouldWait.IsSet() {
145					// 	awg.WaitResponse.Channel <- struct{}{}
146					// }
147	
148					if assumedMsgType, ok := packetSizeToMsgType[size]; ok {
149						junkSize := msgTypeToJunkSize[assumedMsgType]
150						// transport size can align with other header types;
151						// making sure we have the right msgType
152						msgType = binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(packet[junkSize : junkSize+4])
153						if msgType == assumedMsgType {
154							packet = packet[junkSize:]
155						} else {
156							device.log.Verbosef("transport packet lined up with another msg type")
157							msgType = binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(packet[:4])
158						}
159					} else {
160						msgType = binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(packet[:4])
161						if msgType != MessageTransportType {
162							// probably a junk packet
163							device.log.Verbosef("aSec: Received message with unknown type: %d", msgType)
164							continue
165						}
166					}
167				} else {
168					msgType = binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(packet[:4])
169				}
170				switch msgType {
171	
172				// check if transport
173	
174				case MessageTransportType:
175	
176					// check size
177	
178					if len(packet) < MessageTransportSize {
179						continue
180					}
181	
182					// lookup key pair
183	
184					receiver := binary.LittleEndian.Uint32(
185						packet[MessageTransportOffsetReceiver:MessageTransportOffsetCounter],
186					)
187					value := device.indexTable.Lookup(receiver)
188					keypair := value.keypair
189					if keypair == nil {
190						continue
191					}
192	
193					// check keypair expiry
194	
195					if keypair.created.Add(RejectAfterTime).Before(time.Now()) {
196						continue
197					}
198	
199					// create work element
200					peer := value.peer
201					elem := device.GetInboundElement()
202					elem.packet = packet
203					elem.buffer = bufsArrs[i]
204					elem.keypair = keypair
205					elem.endpoint = endpoints[i]
206					elem.counter = 0
207	
208					elemsForPeer, ok := elemsByPeer[peer]
209					if !ok {
210						elemsForPeer = device.GetInboundElementsContainer()
211						elemsForPeer.Lock()
212						elemsByPeer[peer] = elemsForPeer
213					}
214					elemsForPeer.elems = append(elemsForPeer.elems, elem)
215					bufsArrs[i] = device.GetMessageBuffer()
216					bufs[i] = bufsArrs[i][:]
217					continue
218	
219				// otherwise it is a fixed size & handshake related packet
220	
221				case MessageInitiationType:
222					if len(packet) != MessageInitiationSize {
223						continue
224					}
225	
226				case MessageResponseType:
227					if len(packet) != MessageResponseSize {
228						continue
229					}
230	
231				case MessageCookieReplyType:
232					if len(packet) != MessageCookieReplySize {
233						continue
234					}
235	
236				default:
237					device.log.Verbosef("Received message with unknown type")
238					continue
239				}
240	
241				select {
242				case device.queue.handshake.c <- QueueHandshakeElement{
243					msgType:  msgType,
244					buffer:   bufsArrs[i],
245					packet:   packet,
246					endpoint: endpoints[i],
247				}:
248					bufsArrs[i] = device.GetMessageBuffer()
249					bufs[i] = bufsArrs[i][:]
250				default:
251				}
252			}
253			device.awg.ASecMux.RUnlock()
254			for peer, elemsContainer := range elemsByPeer {
255				if peer.isRunning.Load() {
256					peer.queue.inbound.c <- elemsContainer
257					device.queue.decryption.c <- elemsContainer
258				} else {
259					for _, elem := range elemsContainer.elems {
260						device.PutMessageBuffer(elem.buffer)
261						device.PutInboundElement(elem)
262					}
263					device.PutInboundElementsContainer(elemsContainer)
264				}
265				delete(elemsByPeer, peer)
266			}
267		}
268	}
269	
270	func (device *Device) RoutineDecryption(id int) {
271		var nonce [chacha20poly1305.NonceSize]byte
272	
273		defer device.log.Verbosef("Routine: decryption worker %d - stopped", id)
274		device.log.Verbosef("Routine: decryption worker %d - started", id)
275	
276		for elemsContainer := range device.queue.decryption.c {
277			for _, elem := range elemsContainer.elems {
278				// split message into fields
279				counter := elem.packet[MessageTransportOffsetCounter:MessageTransportOffsetContent]
280				content := elem.packet[MessageTransportOffsetContent:]
281	
282				// decrypt and release to consumer
283				var err error
284				elem.counter = binary.LittleEndian.Uint64(counter)
285				// copy counter to nonce
286				binary.LittleEndian.PutUint64(nonce[0x4:0xc], elem.counter)
287				elem.packet, err = elem.keypair.receive.Open(
288					content[:0],
289					nonce[:],
290					content,
291					nil,
292				)
293				if err != nil {
294					elem.packet = nil
295				}
296			}
297			elemsContainer.Unlock()
298		}
299	}
300	
301	/* Handles incoming packets related to handshake
302	 */
303	func (device *Device) RoutineHandshake(id int) {
304		defer func() {
305			device.log.Verbosef("Routine: handshake worker %d - stopped", id)
306			device.queue.encryption.wg.Done()
307		}()
308		device.log.Verbosef("Routine: handshake worker %d - started", id)
309	
310		for elem := range device.queue.handshake.c {
311	
312			device.awg.ASecMux.RLock()
313	
314			// handle cookie fields and ratelimiting
315	
316			switch elem.msgType {
317	
318			case MessageCookieReplyType:
319	
320				// unmarshal packet
321	
322				var reply MessageCookieReply
323				reader := bytes.NewReader(elem.packet)
324				err := binary.Read(reader, binary.LittleEndian, &reply)
325				if err != nil {
326					device.log.Verbosef("Failed to decode cookie reply")
327					goto skip
328				}
329	
330				// lookup peer from index
331	
332				entry := device.indexTable.Lookup(reply.Receiver)
333	
334				if entry.peer == nil {
335					goto skip
336				}
337	
338				// consume reply
339	
340				if peer := entry.peer; peer.isRunning.Load() {
341					device.log.Verbosef(
342						"Receiving cookie response from %s",
343						elem.endpoint.DstToString(),
344					)
345					if !peer.cookieGenerator.ConsumeReply(&reply) {
346						device.log.Verbosef(
347							"Could not decrypt invalid cookie response",
348						)
349					}
350				}
351	
352				goto skip
353	
354			case MessageInitiationType, MessageResponseType:
355	
356				// check mac fields and maybe ratelimit
357	
358				if !device.cookieChecker.CheckMAC1(elem.packet) {
359					device.log.Verbosef("Received packet with invalid mac1")
360					goto skip
361				}
362	
363				// endpoints destination address is the source of the datagram
364	
365				if device.IsUnderLoad() {
366	
367					// verify MAC2 field
368	
369					if !device.cookieChecker.CheckMAC2(elem.packet, elem.endpoint.DstToBytes()) {
370						device.SendHandshakeCookie(&elem)
371						goto skip
372					}
373	
374					// check ratelimiter
375	
376					if !device.rate.limiter.Allow(elem.endpoint.DstIP()) {
377						goto skip
378					}
379				}
380	
381			default:
382				device.log.Errorf("Invalid packet ended up in the handshake queue")
383				goto skip
384			}
385	
386			// handle handshake initiation/response content
387	
388			switch elem.msgType {
389			case MessageInitiationType:
390				// unmarshal
391				var msg MessageInitiation
392				reader := bytes.NewReader(elem.packet)
393				err := binary.Read(reader, binary.LittleEndian, &msg)
394				if err != nil {
395					device.log.Errorf("Failed to decode initiation message")
396					goto skip
397				}
398	
399				// consume initiation
400				peer := device.ConsumeMessageInitiation(&msg)
401				if peer == nil {
402					device.log.Verbosef("Received invalid initiation message from %s", elem.endpoint.DstToString())
403					goto skip
404				}
405	
406				// update timers
407	
408				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketTraversal()
409				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketReceived()
410	
411				// update endpoint
412				peer.SetEndpointFromPacket(elem.endpoint)
413	
414				device.log.Verbosef("%v - Received handshake initiation", peer)
415				peer.rxBytes.Add(uint64(len(elem.packet)))
416	
417				peer.SendHandshakeResponse()
418	
419			case MessageResponseType:
420	
421				// unmarshal
422	
423				var msg MessageResponse
424				reader := bytes.NewReader(elem.packet)
425				err := binary.Read(reader, binary.LittleEndian, &msg)
426				if err != nil {
427					device.log.Errorf("Failed to decode response message")
428					goto skip
429				}
430	
431				// consume response
432	
433				peer := device.ConsumeMessageResponse(&msg)
434				if peer == nil {
435					device.log.Verbosef("Received invalid response message from %s", elem.endpoint.DstToString())
436					goto skip
437				}
438	
439				// update endpoint
440				peer.SetEndpointFromPacket(elem.endpoint)
441	
442				device.log.Verbosef("%v - Received handshake response", peer)
443				peer.rxBytes.Add(uint64(len(elem.packet)))
444	
445				// update timers
446	
447				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketTraversal()
448				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketReceived()
449	
450				// derive keypair
451	
452				err = peer.BeginSymmetricSession()
453	
454				if err != nil {
455					device.log.Errorf("%v - Failed to derive keypair: %v", peer, err)
456					goto skip
457				}
458	
459				peer.timersSessionDerived()
460				peer.timersHandshakeComplete()
461				peer.SendKeepalive()
462			}
463		skip:
464			device.awg.ASecMux.RUnlock()
465			device.PutMessageBuffer(elem.buffer)
466		}
467	}
468	
469	func (peer *Peer) RoutineSequentialReceiver(maxBatchSize int) {
470		device := peer.device
471		defer func() {
472			device.log.Verbosef("%v - Routine: sequential receiver - stopped", peer)
473			peer.stopping.Done()
474		}()
475		device.log.Verbosef("%v - Routine: sequential receiver - started", peer)
476	
477		bufs := make([][]byte, 0, maxBatchSize)
478	
479		for elemsContainer := range peer.queue.inbound.c {
480			if elemsContainer == nil {
481				return
482			}
483			elemsContainer.Lock()
484			validTailPacket := -1
485			dataPacketReceived := false
486			rxBytesLen := uint64(0)
487			for i, elem := range elemsContainer.elems {
488				if elem.packet == nil {
489					// decryption failed
490					continue
491				}
492	
493				if !elem.keypair.replayFilter.ValidateCounter(elem.counter, RejectAfterMessages) {
494					continue
495				}
496	
497				validTailPacket = i
498				if peer.ReceivedWithKeypair(elem.keypair) {
499					peer.SetEndpointFromPacket(elem.endpoint)
500					peer.timersHandshakeComplete()
501					peer.SendStagedPackets()
502				}
503				rxBytesLen += uint64(len(elem.packet) + MinMessageSize)
504	
505				if len(elem.packet) == 0 {
506					device.log.Verbosef("%v - Receiving keepalive packet", peer)
507					continue
508				}
509				dataPacketReceived = true
510	
511				switch elem.packet[0] >> 4 {
512				case 4:
513					if len(elem.packet) < ipv4.HeaderLen {
514						continue
515					}
516					field := elem.packet[IPv4offsetTotalLength : IPv4offsetTotalLength+2]
517					length := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(field)
518					if int(length) > len(elem.packet) || int(length) < ipv4.HeaderLen {
519						continue
520					}
521					elem.packet = elem.packet[:length]
522					src := elem.packet[IPv4offsetSrc : IPv4offsetSrc+net.IPv4len]
523					if device.allowedips.Lookup(src) != peer {
524						device.log.Verbosef("IPv4 packet with disallowed source address from %v", peer)
525						continue
526					}
527	
528				case 6:
529					if len(elem.packet) < ipv6.HeaderLen {
530						continue
531					}
532					field := elem.packet[IPv6offsetPayloadLength : IPv6offsetPayloadLength+2]
533					length := binary.BigEndian.Uint16(field)
534					length += ipv6.HeaderLen
535					if int(length) > len(elem.packet) {
536						continue
537					}
538					elem.packet = elem.packet[:length]
539					src := elem.packet[IPv6offsetSrc : IPv6offsetSrc+net.IPv6len]
540					if device.allowedips.Lookup(src) != peer {
541						device.log.Verbosef("IPv6 packet with disallowed source address from %v", peer)
542						continue
543					}
544	
545				default:
546					device.log.Verbosef(
547						"Packet with invalid IP version from %v",
548						peer,
549					)
550					continue
551				}
552	
553				bufs = append(
554					bufs,
555					elem.buffer[:MessageTransportOffsetContent+len(elem.packet)],
556				)
557			}
558	
559			peer.rxBytes.Add(rxBytesLen)
560			if validTailPacket >= 0 {
561				peer.SetEndpointFromPacket(elemsContainer.elems[validTailPacket].endpoint)
562				peer.keepKeyFreshReceiving()
563				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketTraversal()
564				peer.timersAnyAuthenticatedPacketReceived()
565			}
566			if dataPacketReceived {
567				peer.timersDataReceived()
568			}
569			if len(bufs) > 0 {
570				_, err := device.tun.device.Write(bufs, MessageTransportOffsetContent)
571				if err != nil && !device.isClosed() {
572					device.log.Errorf("Failed to write packets to TUN device: %v", err)
573				}
574			}
575			for _, elem := range elemsContainer.elems {
576				device.PutMessageBuffer(elem.buffer)
577				device.PutInboundElement(elem)
578			}
579			bufs = bufs[:0]
580			device.PutInboundElementsContainer(elemsContainer)
581		}
582	}
583
